title: Apex Area Technical Corrections Act
plain_summary: This law fixes problems with an older 1989 law about moving federal land in Nevada to local groups. The original law only let Clark County get the land and rights to connect utilities like water and electricity. Now the City of North Las Vegas and an industrial park group can also get this land and utility connection rights. The land is meant to be used for heavy industry, including businesses that handle dangerous materials. The federal government will keep control over some parts of the land forever.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Apex Area Technical Corrections Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Apex Project, Nevada Land Transfer and Authorization Act of 1989
sections.text: This section makes changes to an older law from nineteen eighty-nine called the Apex Project, Nevada Land Transfer and Authorization Act. The changes add new definitions and update existing rules. First, it adds a definition for the Apex Industrial Park Owners Association, which is a group that was created on April ninth, two thousand and one, according to documents filed with the Nevada Secretary of State. Second, it defines the City of North Las Vegas as North Las Vegas, Nevada. The section also changes the rules about who can connect utilities and infrastructure to federal land. Instead of only Clark County having this right, now Clark County, the City of North Las Vegas, and the Apex Industrial Park Owners Association can all work together or separately to make these connections. The changes also expand the areas where these connections can be made to include not just the Kerr-McGee Site but also other lands that are transferred under this law. The section updates references to maps by allowing the Secretary to create new successor maps. It changes who gets easements and rights-of-way from just Clark County to include the City of North Las Vegas and the Apex Industrial Park Owners Association as well. The section makes the withdrawal of certain lands permanent for all lands transferred under this law. It creates new rules for selling mineral materials that come from construction work like grading or land balancing on parcels where the United States still owns the mineral rights. For these sales, the government does not need to seek competitive bids, and normal limits on quantity and time periods do not apply. Finally, it adds a requirement that any future land transfers or rights-of-way must follow federal environmental laws, including the National Environmental Policy Act of nineteen sixty-nine and the Federal Land Policy and Management Act of nineteen seventy-six.
